entries.label: Septimius Severus
entries.hint: Founded the Severan dynasty after winning the civil war of 193; died at York on campaign in Britain.
entries.note: Gold aureus coinage extensively documented. SHA Vita Severi. Died Eboracum (York) 4 Feb 211 — Cassius Dio 77.15 records date.

entries.label: Caracalla
entries.loc: Rome; campaigns on Rhine, Danube, and Parthian frontier
entries.hint: Murdered his brother Geta to rule alone; issued the Constitutio Antoniniana granting citizenship to all free inhabitants of the Empire.
entries.note: Constitutio Antoniniana (212) survives as P.Giss. 40 (Papyrus Giessen). Murdered 8 Apr 217 near Carrhae — Cassius Dio 79.4.

entries.label: Macrinus
entries.hint: First non-senatorial, non-Italian emperor; praetorian prefect who arranged Caracalla's murder then seized power.
entries.note: Coinage series confirms reign. Cassius Dio 79. Defeated and executed 218 — Herodian 5.4.

entries.label: Elagabalus
entries.hint: Syrian priest-emperor who imposed his sun god cult on Rome; his religious innovations provoked the praetorian guard to murder him.
entries.note: SHA Vita Heliogabali. Murdered 11 Mar 222 — Cassius Dio 80.20. Extensive numismatic record.

entries.label: Severus Alexander
entries.loc: Rome; campaigns on Rhine and against Sassanids
entries.hint: Last of the Severan dynasty; murdered by his own troops on the Rhine, triggering the Crisis of the Third Century.
entries.note: SHA Vita Alexandri. Murdered at Mogontiacum (Mainz) Mar 235 — Herodian 6.9. Extensive legal record in the Codex Justinianus.

entries.label: Maximinus Thrax
entries.loc: Rhine frontier campaigns; never visited Rome
entries.hint: First emperor never to set foot in Rome; a Thracian peasant soldier elevated by the army, killed by his own troops after 3 years.
entries.note: SHA Vita Maximini. Killed at Aquileia 238 — Herodian 8.5. Coinage confirms reign, minted without Senate recognition.

entries.label: Gordian I & II
entries.hint: A 79-year-old senator and his son proclaimed in Africa; both dead within 3 weeks — Gordian II killed in battle, Gordian I by suicide.
entries.note: SHA Vita Gordianorum. Herodian 7.5–9. Coinage extremely rare — confirms 21-day reign. Died Mar 238.

entries.label: Pupienus & Balbinus
entries.hint: Two senatorial emperors elected simultaneously by the Senate to oppose Maximinus; both murdered by the praetorian guard in 99 days.
entries.note: SHA Vita Maximi et Balbini. Herodian 8.6–8. Murdered Jul 238. Numismatic record confirms brief joint reign.

entries.label: Gordian III
entries.hint: 13-year-old elevated by the praetorians after they killed Pupienus and Balbinus; died on campaign against Shapur I.
entries.note: SHA Vita Gordiani III. Died Feb 244 near Ctesiphon — circumstances disputed; Res Gestae Divi Saporis (inscription) records Shapur's version.

entries.label: Philip the Arab
entries.hint: First emperor from the province of Arabia; presided over Rome's 1,000th anniversary celebrations in 248.
entries.note: SHA Vita Philippi. Rome Millennium coinage (248 AD) well documented — extensive numismatic series. Killed at Verona Sep 249 — Eutropius 9.3.

entries.label: Decius
entries.hint: First emperor to die in battle against a foreign enemy; killed fighting the Goths at the Battle of Abritus, 251 AD.
entries.note: Killed at Abritus (mod. Razgrad, Bulgaria) Jun 251 — Zosimus 1.23. Issued empire-wide sacrifice edict (250) attested in multiple papyri: P.Oxy. 658, P.Mich. 3867.

entries.label: Gallienus
entries.hint: Longest-reigning Crisis emperor; faced simultaneous invasions, the Gallic breakaway, the Palmyrene breakaway, and multiple usurpers.
entries.note: Extensive coinage. SHA Vita Gallieni. Murdered at Milan 268 — Zosimus 1.40. His military reforms documented by Zosimus and later sources.

entries.label: Valerian
entries.hint: Only Roman emperor ever captured alive by an enemy — taken prisoner by Shapur I of Persia at the Battle of Edessa, 260 AD.
entries.note: Capture attested in Res Gestae Divi Saporis, rock inscription at Naqsh-e Rostam (Iran, extant). Also Zosimus 1.36, Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 5.

entries.label: Claudius Gothicus
entries.hint: Defeated the Goths at the Battle of Naissus (269), earning his cognomen; died of plague shortly after — the first of the Illyrian restorer emperors.
entries.note: SHA Vita Claudii. Battle of Naissus: Zosimus 1.43–45. Died of plague Sirmium 270 — confirmed numismatically and in Eutropius 9.11.

entries.label: Aurelian
entries.hint: 'Restorer of the World' — reunified the empire by defeating the Gallic Empire (274) and Palmyra (273); built the Aurelian Wall around Rome.
entries.note: SHA Vita Aureliani. Aurelian Walls (begun 271): extant, inscriptions survive. Reunification coinage: RESTITVTOR ORBIS series. Murdered 275 — SHA.

entries.label: Probus
entries.hint: Systematic restorer of the Rhine and Danube frontiers; employed barbarian federates at scale — a policy that defined the late empire.
entries.note: SHA Vita Probi. Murdered at Sirmium 282 — SHA. Coinage well attested. Policy on barbarian federates documented in SHA and later Zosimus.

entries.label: Diocletian
entries.hint: Ended the Crisis definitively; created the Tetrarchy (rule of four), reformed coinage, tax, army, and administration — reshaping the empire entirely.
entries.note: Edict on Maximum Prices (301): over 1,000 fragments found across Eastern Mediterranean. Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 7–18. Palace at Split (Croatia): extant, UNESCO.

entries.label: Battle of Lugdunum
entries.hint: Septimius Severus crushes his last rival Clodius Albinus; the largest Roman civil war battle since Actium.
entries.note: Cassius Dio 76.6–7. Herodian 3.7. Site near modern Lyon confirmed by coin finds and Cassius Dio's topographic description.

entries.label: Severus' Parthian campaigns
entries.hint: Severus sacks the Parthian capital Ctesiphon for the third time in Roman history, creating the new province of Mesopotamia.
entries.note: Cassius Dio 76.9. Arch of Septimius Severus (Rome, 203 AD, extant): reliefs record the Parthian campaigns explicitly.

entries.label: Severus' British campaign
entries.hint: Severus personally leads the last major Roman offensive into Scotland; dies at York before the campaign concludes.
entries.note: Cassius Dio 77.11–15. Herodian 3.14–15. Severus died at Eboracum 4 Feb 211 — SHA and Cassius Dio agree on date.

entries.label: Battle of the Abritus
entries.hint: Emperor Decius and his son killed fighting the Goths — the first Roman emperor to die in battle against a foreign enemy.
entries.note: Zosimus 1.23. Dexippus (fragments in FHG III). Coins of Decius struck 249–251 provide precise terminus. Site confirmed by modern archaeology.

entries.label: Battle of Edessa — Valerian captured
entries.hint: Emperor Valerian captured alive by Shapur I of Persia — the most humiliating defeat in Roman history; he died in Persian captivity.
entries.note: Primary: Res Gestae Divi Saporis, rock inscription at Naqsh-e Rostam (Iran). Also Zosimus 1.36; Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 5. Date disputed: 259 or 260.

entries.label: Gothic invasions of the Balkans
entries.hint: Gothic sea-raids and land invasions devastate Greece and Asia Minor; Athens sacked c.267, Ephesus temples looted.
entries.note: Zosimus 1.39–43. Dexippus: Scythica fragments (P.Oxy. 5105, discovered 2010 — eyewitness account of Athenian defence). Destruction layers confirmed archaeologically.

entries.label: Battle of Naissus
entries.hint: Claudius Gothicus defeats a massive Gothic force near Naissus, breaking the Gothic threat to the Balkans — his cognomen 'Gothicus' follows.
entries.note: Zosimus 1.43–45. SHA Vita Claudii 8–9. Scale of battle (reported 320,000 Goths) likely exaggerated; victory confirmed by coinage and title.

entries.label: Zenobia's conquest of Egypt and Asia
entries.hint: Queen Zenobia of Palmyra seizes Egypt and much of Asia Minor, creating a breakaway Palmyrene Empire that controls Rome's eastern trade.
entries.note: Historia Augusta Vita Aureliani 22–31. Zosimus 1.44–50. Egyptian papyri show Palmyrene administrative control — P.Oxy. 1413 dated by Vaballathus era.

entries.label: Aurelian defeats Palmyra
entries.hint: Aurelian defeats Zenobia twice — at Immae and outside Palmyra — then sacks the city after a second revolt, ending the Palmyrene Empire.
entries.note: SHA Vita Aureliani 25–28. Zosimus 1.50–56. Palmyra ruins (extant, UNESCO) show destruction layer. Coins of Vaballathus/Zenobia give chronology.

entries.label: Aurelian defeats the Gallic Empire
entries.hint: Aurelian defeats Tetricus at the Battle of Châlons, reunifying the breakaway Gallic Empire with Rome after 14 years of separation.
entries.note: SHA Vita Aureliani 32–39. Zosimus 1.61. Tetricus's surrender in 274: Eutropius 9.13 records the triumphal procession in Rome.

entries.label: Constitutio Antoniniana
entries.loc: Issued Empire-wide (papyrus found at Giessen, Germany)
entries.hint: Caracalla's edict extending Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the Empire — the most sweeping legal act in Roman history.
entries.note: P.Giss. 40 (Papyrus Giessen): partial text survives, recto and verso. Cassius Dio 78.9 mentions it. Legal implications documented in Digest of Justinian.

entries.label: Decius' Empire-wide sacrifice edict
entries.loc: Issued Empire-wide; libelli (certificates) found in Egypt
entries.hint: First systematic empire-wide persecution of Christians: all inhabitants must sacrifice to the gods and obtain a certificate (libellus).
entries.note: Forty-seven libelli survive on papyrus from Egypt (P.Oxy. 658, P.Mich. 3867 etc.). Cyprian of Carthage's correspondence (letters 8–12) is contemporary.

entries.label: Edict of Maximum Prices (Diocletian)
entries.loc: Issued Nicomedia; fragments found across Eastern Mediterranean
entries.hint: Diocletian's attempt to fix maximum prices for goods and wages across the Empire — the most detailed ancient price document to survive.
entries.note: Over 1,000 fragments found in Turkey, Greece, Jordan, Italy. Reconstructed by Lauffer (1971). Latin and Greek versions. Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 7.6 criticises it.

entries.label: Tetrarchy established
entries.hint: Diocletian formally creates the rule of four (two Augusti, two Caesars), dividing the empire's military and administrative burden.
entries.note: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 8. Panegyrici Latini III (289) and VI (307) document the system. Tetrarchic portraits: porphyry group, St Mark's Venice (extant).

entries.label: Diocletian's currency reform
entries.hint: Systematic overhaul of the Roman coinage — new silver argenteus and reformed bronze — attempting to stabilise decades of currency debasement.
entries.note: Numismatic evidence: mint-marks on reformed coins (RIC V-VI). The argenteus series: Carradice & Buttrey (2007). Lactantius criticises the financial effects.

entries.label: Diocletian's provincial reorganisation
entries.hint: Diocletian doubles the number of provinces (from ~50 to ~100) and creates a new layer of dioceses — reshaping Roman administration for centuries.
entries.note: Attested in the Laterculus Veronensis (Verona List, c.314) — earliest surviving list of all Roman provinces. Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 7.4.

entries.label: Cult of Sol Invictus promoted (Elagabalus)
entries.hint: Emperor Elagabalus imports his Syrian sun god cult to Rome, building a new temple and attempting to make it supreme — later reversed by Alexander Severus.
entries.note: SHA Vita Heliogabali 3.1–5. Cassius Dio 80.11. Coins show the conical baetyl (sacred stone) being carried to Rome. Temple site on Palatine identified archaeologically.

entries.label: Origen writes Contra Celsum
entries.hint: The most sophisticated Christian intellectual defence against pagan philosophy — written in response to the philosopher Celsus's attack on Christianity.
entries.note: Manuscript tradition: earliest complete MS 13th c. (Vaticanus gr. 386). Written c.248 — internal reference to 'almost 200 years since Jesus'. Jerome, De Viris Ill. 54.

entries.label: Decian persecution of Christians
entries.loc: Empire-wide (documented in Egypt, North Africa, Rome)
entries.hint: First systematic empire-wide persecution requiring all citizens to sacrifice to Roman gods; drives Cyprian of Carthage into hiding.
entries.note: Cyprian of Carthage: Letters 8–12 (written during persecution, dated by context). Forty-seven libelli (sacrifice certificates) survive on papyrus from Egypt.

entries.label: Valerian's persecution of Christians
entries.hint: Two edicts targeting specifically the Church leadership and property; Cyprian of Carthage beheaded 14 Sep 258, the first bishop-martyr.
entries.note: Cyprian: Letters 75–82 (258). Eusebius HE 7.10–13 preserves Valerian's letters. Acta Cypriani (eyewitness account of martyrdom, 258) survives in full.

entries.label: Mani founds Manichaeism
entries.hint: The prophet Mani begins preaching his synthesis of Zoroastrian, Christian and Buddhist ideas — a religion that will spread from Spain to China.
entries.note: Mani's own writings: Cologne Mani Codex (CMC), papyrus found 1969, now Cologne University — earliest autobiographical source. Executed by Bahram I c.274–277.

entries.label: Diocletian's Great Persecution begins
entries.hint: The most systematic persecution of Christians in Roman history: churches destroyed, scriptures burned, clergy imprisoned — begins on the Feast of Terminalia.
entries.note: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 12–15 (eyewitness; Lactantius was in Nicomedia). Eusebius HE 8.2–6. First edict dated 23 Feb 303 by Lactantius.

entries.label: Arch of Septimius Severus, Rome
entries.hint: Triumphal arch celebrating Severus's Parthian victories; reliefs document the campaigns in explicit detail — still standing in the Forum.
entries.note: Extant. Dedicatory inscription survives (CIL VI.1033). Construction date 203 confirmed by inscription. Reliefs: Brilliant (1967), Arch of Septimius Severus.

entries.label: Baths of Caracalla, Rome
entries.hint: The largest bathing complex in the Roman world, accommodating 1,600 bathers; the concrete vaulting remains one of antiquity's greatest engineering feats.
entries.note: Extant (partial). Building inscription CIL VI.1082. Dedication confirmed 216 by Chronographer of 354. Floor mosaics (athletes): now Vatican Museums.

entries.label: Aurelian Walls of Rome begun
entries.hint: 12-metre-high brick wall encircling Rome for the first time since the Republic — a monument to the city's vulnerability in the Crisis years.
entries.note: Mostly extant. SHA Vita Aureliani 21.9. Brickstamps dated 271–275 by Steinby (1986). Later heightened by Maxentius (306–312) — structurally distinguishable.

entries.label: Ludovisi Battle Sarcophagus
entries.loc: Found: Rome · Now: Palazzo Altemps, Rome
entries.hint: Marble sarcophagus with a frenzied battle scene between Romans and 'barbarians' — the most vivid sculptural image of the Crisis-era military anxiety.
entries.note: Palazzo Altemps, Rome (Inv. 8574). No inscription but dated stylistically and by material context to c.250–260. Kähler (1952); Andreae (1956).

entries.label: Portraiture: the tetrarchic porphyry group
entries.loc: Constantinople (origin) · Now: St Mark's Basilica, Venice
entries.hint: Four porphyry emperors embracing each other — the definitive image of the Tetrarchy: rigid, abstract, deliberately non-individual.
entries.note: St Mark's Basilica, Venice (spolia from Constantinople). Porphyry quarried at Mons Porphyrites, Egypt. Companion pieces: Vatican Museums. Dated c.300 on stylistic and historical grounds.

entries.label: Palace of Diocletian, Split
entries.hint: Diocletian's retirement palace — a fortified city for a single man; still inhabited today, with thousands of people living inside its walls.
entries.note: Extant, UNESCO. Construction dated 295–305 by brick stamps and historical sources. Malalas and Eutropius record retirement there. Hebrard & Zeiller survey (1912).

entries.label: The hydraulis (water organ) in Roman public life
entries.loc: Amphitheatres and arenas across the empire; Aquincum (Budapest) find
entries.hint: The water-powered pipe organ accompanies gladiatorial games and theatrical performances across the empire — the most technologically sophisticated musical instrument of antiquity, described by Vitruvius and confirmed by the Aquincum organ fragments found in 1931.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Aquincum organ fragments (Budapest History Museum, inv. 64.10.1): bronze components of a hydraulis dated 228 CE by inscription 'Gaius Iulius Viatorinus... anno CCXXVIII.' Vitruvius, De Architectura X.8: detailed technical description. Hero of Alexandria, Pneumatica 1.42: independent technical account. McKinnon (2001), The Temple, the Church Fathers and Early Western Chant. The 228 CE inscription makes the Aquincum find the only precisely dated musical instrument from the Roman world.

entries.label: Cassius Dio: Roman History
entries.loc: Written in Rome and Bithynia (author's origin: Nicaea)
entries.hint: 80-book history of Rome from Aeneas to 229 AD; Dio was a senator and eyewitness to the Severan court — Books 78–80 are primary sources.
entries.note: Partially preserved: Books 36–54 complete, others in epitome (Xiphilinus, 11th c.). Oldest MS: Codex Venetus Marcianus gr. 395 (11th c.). Author identifies himself as witness.

entries.label: Herodian: History of the Empire
entries.loc: Written in Rome (author's exact origin unknown)
entries.hint: 8-book history covering 180–238 AD, written by a minor bureaucrat who claims to have witnessed some events — our main source for 193–238.
entries.note: Complete text survives. Oldest MS: Codex Laurentianus 70.7 (11th c.). Author's claim to eyewitness status (1.1.3) taken seriously by most scholars.

entries.label: Cyprian of Carthage: Letters
entries.hint: Bishop of Carthage writes 82 surviving letters during the Decian and Valerian persecutions — the most vivid eyewitness account of Christians under pressure.
entries.note: 82 letters survive in multiple MS traditions (oldest: 6th c. Codex Parisinus lat. 1661). The corpus is precisely dated: letters reference events we can fix independently.

entries.label: Dexippus: Scythica (fragments)
entries.hint: Athenian historian who personally organised the defence of Athens against Gothic raiders c.267; his fragments are the only near-contemporary Greek account of the invasions.
entries.note: P.Oxy. 5105 (discovered 2010, published 2016) — new fragment showing Dexippus's Athenian speech. Older fragments: FHG III. Millar (1969) is the key study.

entries.label: Res Gestae Divi Saporis (Shapur I inscription)
entries.hint: Shapur I's own account of his victories over three Roman emperors — the primary source for Valerian's capture, written in Middle Persian, Parthian and Greek.
entries.note: Rock inscription at Naqsh-e Rostam (extant). Three languages: Middle Persian, Parthian, Greek. Published by Sprengling (1953); Back (1978). Unique as a non-Roman primary source for Roman history.

entries.label: Lactantius: De Mortibus Persecutorum
entries.hint: Eyewitness account of Diocletian's Great Persecution and the Tetrarchic crisis — Lactantius was in Nicomedia when the persecution began in 303.
entries.note: Lactantius was present at Nicomedia. Text survives complete in Codex Bononiensis 701 (11th c.). Internal dating references allow precise chronology. Creed (1984) critical edition.

entries.label: Currency debasement crisis
entries.loc: Empire-wide (mint evidence from Rome, Antioch, Alexandria)
entries.hint: The silver content of the antoninianus coin falls from ~50% to under 2% between 235 and 270 — the most severe monetary debasement in Roman history.
entries.note: Numismatic analysis: Walker (1976–1978), The Metrology of the Roman Silver Coinage. XRF analysis of coin hoards. The Antioch hoard (Reece 1999) provides the debasement curve.

entries.label: Plague of Cyprian
entries.hint: A devastating epidemic kills thousands daily across the empire for over a decade — Cyprian of Carthage describes symptoms in De Mortalitate (252).
entries.note: Cyprian: De Mortalitate (252 AD) — primary source, names symptoms. Eusebius HE 7.22. Pontius, Vita Cypriani 9. Pathogen identity debated: haemorrhagic fever, smallpox, or Ebola-like virus (Kyle Harper, 2017).

entries.label: Antonine plague long-term demographic impact
entries.hint: The earlier Antonine plague (165–180) left a demographic hole that shaped 3rd century labour shortages — papyri from Egypt show declining village populations.
entries.note: Egyptian census papyri (P.Oxy. various): Bagnall & Frier, The Demography of Roman Egypt (1994). Scheidel (2002) synthesises the evidence for population decline.

entries.label: Baths of Caracalla — concrete engineering
entries.hint: The largest concrete vaulted spaces in antiquity — the 55m-span main hall was not surpassed until the 19th century.
entries.note: Extant. Vault dimensions: Lancaster (2005), Concrete Vaulted Construction in Imperial Rome. Opus caementicium (Roman concrete) composition analysed: Jackson et al. (2013).

entries.label: Aurelian Walls — rapid military construction
entries.hint: 19km of brick-faced concrete walls built in under 5 years — an astonishing engineering achievement by a workforce that included soldiers and civilians.
entries.note: Brickstamps dated by Steinby (1986). Construction pace inferred from SHA and numismatic dating. Coarelli (1999) on construction method.

entries.label: Roman road network — maintenance documentation
entries.hint: Milestones from the 3rd century — many bearing emperor names — are among the most numerous surviving inscriptions, documenting road maintenance even during the Crisis.
entries.note: Milestone corpus: AE (L'Année Épigraphique) annual volumes. 3rd-century milestones the most numerous Roman period — Witschel (2002) analyses 2,000+ examples.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Diplomacy, trade routes, cultural transmission & the movement of peoples, goods and ideas

entries.label: Sassanid disruption of eastern trade under Shapur I
entries.hint: Sassanid expansion and Roman military collapse disrupt the Silk Road through Palmyra — Palmyrene trade inscriptions (the PAT corpus) end abruptly c.273 after Aurelian's sack, the clearest epigraphic marker of a trade network's death.
entries.note: Palmyrene Aramaic Trade inscriptions (PAT corpus): Hillers & Cussini (1996). Inscriptions end c.273 — the corpus itself is the evidence of disruption. Dramatic decline in eastern luxury goods in Western hoards post-260: documented numismatically. MOVED from Subsistence s3.

entries.label: Shapur I's diplomatic use of Roman prisoners
entries.hint: After capturing Valerian's army, Shapur I settles Roman prisoners as urban craftsmen, engineers and physicians — a deliberate transfer of Roman technical knowledge into the Sassanid Empire documented in both inscription and archaeology.
entries.note: ŠKZ records Roman captives settled in Persia. Bishapur city's Roman grid plan confirmed archaeologically: Ghirshman (1971–78). Band-e Kaisar dam built with Roman engineering: extant. Gundeshapur's early scholarly tradition linked to prisoner physicians. This is the most concrete 3rd-century evidence of forced cultural transmission.

entries.label: Palmyra as trade entrepôt — the PAT inscriptions
entries.hint: Palmyra's unique bilingual (Greek-Aramaic) caravan inscriptions record individual merchants, their goods and routes — the richest single-site evidence for Silk Road trade in the Roman world, spanning a century until Aurelian's sack ends the corpus.
entries.note: Palmyrene Aramaic inscriptions (PAT): Hillers & Cussini (1996) — the definitive corpus. Tariff inscription of 137 CE (extant, Hermitage): lists goods taxed at Palmyra. Matthews (1984), The Tax Law of Palmyra, JRS 74. The corpus ends abruptly 273 CE — the trade disruption is visible in the epigraphy.

entries.label: Mani's syncretic mission — cross-cultural religion
entries.hint: Mani deliberately synthesises Zoroastrian, Christian and Buddhist traditions into a new universal religion — the most ambitious act of conscious cross-cultural religious synthesis in late antiquity, designed from the outset to travel across all existing boundaries.
entries.note: Cologne Mani Codex (CMC): Mani's own account of his synthesis. His missions documented in Manichaean texts from Turfan (Central Asia) — Allberry (1938). The westward spread after his execution: documented in Augustin's Confessions (he was Manichaean for 9 years). The eastward spread to China: Lieu (1985), Manichaeism in the Later Roman Empire and Medieval China.

entries.label: Rise of the Sassanid Empire
entries.hint: The Sassanids overthrow the Parthians (224) and immediately become a far more aggressive eastern neighbour — Shapur I captures a Roman emperor within 36 years.
entries.note: Ardashir's victory over Artabanus V (224): Tabari (9th c.) and Res Gestae Divi Saporis. Shapur I's rock inscriptions at Naqsh-e Rostam (extant) document the new empire's power.

entries.label: Plague of Cyprian
entries.loc: Empire-wide (Africa, Egypt, Rome, Gaul all documented)
entries.hint: Epidemic mortality on a scale that stripped the empire of defenders, taxpayers and farmers precisely when the external military pressure was greatest.
entries.note: Kyle Harper, The Fate of Rome (2017) synthesises evidence. Cyprian De Mortalitate (252) primary source. DNA evidence for disease pathogen sought but not yet confirmed.

entries.label: Gothic confederation formation
entries.loc: Pontic steppe and Danube delta (modern Ukraine/Romania)
entries.hint: Previously scattered Germanic peoples coalesce into the Gothic confederation north of the Danube — creating the empire's most dangerous northern neighbour.
entries.note: Jordanes, Getica 16–21 (6th c. but preserves earlier tradition). Archaeological: Chernyakhiv culture emergence c.200–250, Heather (1996). Herwig Wolfram, History of the Goths.

entries.label: Breakaway Gallic Empire
entries.hint: After Valerian's capture, the Rhine commander Postumus breaks away and creates the Gallic Empire — a separate Roman state that lasts 14 years.
entries.note: Coinage of Postumus and successors (RIC V). König (1981). Trier as capital: Julian, Oration I (panegyric of Constantius). Reunified by Aurelian 274 — Eutropius 9.13.

entries.label: Breakaway Palmyrene Empire
entries.hint: After Valerian's capture, Palmyra under Odaenathus then Zenobia seizes the eastern provinces — a second breakaway state controlling Rome's eastern trade.
entries.note: Palmyrene coins of Vaballathus/Zenobia. P.Oxy. 1413 (papyrus dated by Palmyrene era) confirms Egyptian control. Zosimus 1.39–50. Destroyed by Aurelian 273.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Ulpian defines the legal personhood of enslaved people
entries.hint: The jurist Ulpian writes the foundational texts on the legal non-personhood of enslaved people — simultaneously the most detailed Roman documentation of their humanity and its systematic denial.
entries.note: Ulpian's texts survive in the Digest of Justinian (533 CE). Digest 1.5.4: 'slavery is an institution of the law of nations by which a person is, contrary to nature, subjected to the ownership of another.' The contradictions in the legal texts — acknowledging humanity while denying legal personality — are the richest source for enslaved life. Watson (1987), Roman Slave Law.

entries.label: Caracalla extends citizenship — Lex Scantinia implications
entries.hint: The Constitutio Antoniniana extending citizenship to all free persons also extends the Lex Scantinia's prohibitions — the law punishing the passive role in same-sex acts between free men now applies to millions of new citizens, redefining who is subject to its gender-normative logic.
entries.note: P.Giss. 40 (Papyrus Giessen): the Constitutio text. Lex Scantinia: Williams (2010), Roman Homosexuality, ch.4. The Scantinia's logic is about status and role (passive = degrading for a free man) not identity — the key distinction from later Christian frameworks. The citizenship extension's legal implications: Sherwin-White (1973).

entries.label: Elagabalus — gender identity in imperial sources
entries.hint: The Historia Augusta describes Elagabalus preferring to be addressed as domina (mistress) rather than dominus (master), wearing women's dress at court, and reportedly seeking physicians to create female genitalia — the most detailed ancient account of what modern scholars read as gender variance at the highest level.
entries.note: SHA Vita Heliogabali 5.1–4 (hostile source — anachronism and bias must be acknowledged). Cassius Dio 80.13–16. The SHA's reliability is disputed, but the consistency of the gender details across hostile sources is notable. Icks (2012), The Crimes of Elagabalus. The anachronism of applying 'transgender' retrospectively: discussed in Roller (2018), Gender Studies and Ancient Rome.

entries.label: Julia Domna — philosopher-empress and intellectual patron
entries.hint: Julia Domna, wife of Septimius Severus, convenes a philosophical circle, corresponds with Philostratus (who dedicates his Life of Apollonius to her), and exercises documented political influence — the best-evidenced example of female intellectual power in the Severan period.
entries.note: Philostratus, Life of Apollonius: dedication to Julia Domna is explicit. Her correspondence and intellectual circle: Levick (2007), Julia Domna: Syrian Empress. Coins depict her as Mater Senatus and Mater Patriae — titles with political content. Her role after Septimius's death: Herodian 4.3.

entries.label: Paul's Letter to the Romans — desire as moral category
entries.loc: Circulating in Rome and across the empire
entries.hint: Paul's Romans 1:26–27 introduces a fundamentally new framework: same-sex desire itself — not just the passive role or citizen status — is the moral problem. This is the CATEGORICAL SHIFT from Roman act-based to Christian desire-based analysis, circulating widely by the 3rd century.
entries.note: Romans 1:26–27: earliest MS Papyrus 46 (Chester Beatty, c.200 CE — within this module's period). Boswell (1980), Christianity, Social Tolerance, and Homosexuality: the foundational (if contested) study. Martin (1995), Heterosexism and the Interpretation of Romans 1:18–32 for the debate. The shift from Roman 'acts' to Christian 'desires' as moral categories: Foucault, The Use of Pleasure (1985).
